intro_band.agent_remit_summary: OpenHands is an autonomous software-engineering agent that accepts natural-language tasks — bug reports, feature requests, issue resolution — and completes them end-to-end by writing code, executing it, browsing the web, and operating on connected version-control and issue-tracker accounts (GitHub, Bitbucket, GitLab, Azure DevOps, Forgejo). All agent-generated code is supposed to run inside a sandboxed runtime (Docker by default), with file I/O confined to the per-session workspace; the task prompt, web content, issue/PR text, and repo file contents are all to be treated as untrusted. The remit requires human confirmation before cross-repository writes, PR merges, force-push/branch-delete/history-rewrite, committing dependency changes, editing CI/workflow files, adding MCP servers at runtime, or connecting new integration accounts, and treats destructive integration operations (repo delete, webhook removal, collaborator revocation) as entirely out of scope.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: The staged workspace is the OpenHands V1 app_server — a FastAPI orchestration layer (openhands/app_server/app.py, v1_router.py) that manages sandboxed conversations, sandboxes (Docker / subprocess / remote), event storage, secrets, settings, and Git/issue-tracker integrations. The agentic core itself — the CodeAct/planning agent loop, prompt construction, sandbox-internal command execution, and the MCP client — is not in this repo; it ships as the pinned openhands-sdk==1.21.1 / openhands-agent-server==1.21.1 / openhands-tools==1.21.1 packages, so this scan can only evaluate the orchestration surface. Notable external surface: REST API under /api/v1/*; an embedded FastMCP server (openhands/app_server/mcp/mcp_router.py) exposing create_pr/create_mr/create_bitbucket_pr/etc. plus a proxied Tavily search; webhook callbacks from agent-server containers; docker.sock mounted into the app container per docker-compose.yml; secrets/keys persisted as files under ~/.openhands. Untrusted issue/PR/comment text is rendered straight into the agent task prompt via Jinja2 templates under integrations/templates/resolver/, and .openhands/setup.sh + .openhands/pre-commit.sh + .openhands/hooks.json from connected repos are executed inside the sandbox at conversation start.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is declared approval gates with no enforcement at the layer Praxa can see: the remit requires human confirmation before cross-repository writes, PR merges, force-push/history-rewrite, CI/workflow file edits, committing dependency changes, runtime MCP-server additions, and new integration grants — but the app_server contains no interposition for any of these. The MCP tools create_pr/create_mr/create_bitbucket_pr in mcp/mcp_router.py open pull requests with the user's provider token and zero confirmation step; confirmation_mode defaults to false in config.template.toml; and the deterministic exec/tool gating that would back the "reject shell commands that reach outside the sandbox" rule lives in the openhands-sdk package that is not part of this repo.

Two secondary themes compound that: (1) untrusted external content — GitHub/GitLab/Jira issue bodies, titles, and PR comments — is rendered straight into the agent's task prompt by the Jinja2 templates under integrations/templates/resolver/ with no origin-labeling or sanitization, putting an indirect-injection ingress directly upstream of the agent's tool-calling loop; and (2) in OSS/local mode the /api/v1/* API has no authentication at all (DefaultUserAuth.get_user_id() returns None, get_dependencies() adds nothing unless SESSION_API_KEY is set) and CORS falls back to allow-any-origin. The codebase does carry real controls — running-state-gated session-key auth, validate_secret_name/validate_secrets_dict, time-limited LookupSecret token references instead of static creds in the agent, a structured event store, and pervasive log-side secret redaction — so this is a mature system with genuine safeguards, not theatre; the gap is that the remit's most consequential promises (the approval gates) and its most dangerous ingress (resolver-fed untrusted text) are unmet at this layer.

rules.rule_text: Execute all agent-generated code inside the sandboxed runtime — never on the host directly
Confine all file reads and writes to the per-session sandbox workspace; reject absolute paths outside the workspace, symlinks that escape it, and `..` traversal
Treat the user's task prompt, web-retrieved page content, issue descriptions, PR comments, and repository file contents as untrusted input

Apply input validation and output filtering at the boundary where LLM output drives tool calls — validate tool-argument shapes, clamp numeric parameters, and reject shell commands that reach outside the sandbox
Verify OAuth tokens, integration PATs, and session tokens on every request to integration APIs — do not cache long-lived credentials in LLM-reachable memory
Record every action the agent takes (tool invoked, arguments, outcome, timestamp) to the session's event log
Enforce per-session wall-clock and step-count caps; halt the agent cleanly when exceeded
Execute agent-generated code on the host OS outside the sandboxed runtime
Read, write, or reference files on the host filesystem outside the sandbox workspace

Follow instructions embedded in web pages / issue descriptions / source files / microagent content that attempt to exfiltrate credentials, escalate out of the sandbox, redirect actions to a different repository, commit or push without user confirmation, or open PRs/close issues beyond the current session's authorized scope
Commit secrets, credentials, tokens, or `.env` contents to any git branch

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — must NEVER do / Human approval required
rules.rule_text: Execute destructive git operations (force-push, branch delete, history rewrite) without user confirmation
Execute destructive integration operations (delete repository, remove webhooks, revoke collaborator access) at any time — out of scope, not approval-gated
Leak API keys or session tokens into logs, error messages, or LLM context
Allow one session's state, memory, or credentials to leak into another session (tenant isolation in cloud and enterprise deployments)
Cross-repository or cross-organization writes — the user must confirm when the agent proposes to modify a repo other than the one the task originated in
Merging pull requests
CI/CD or workflow file edits (`.github/workflows/*`, Jenkinsfiles, CircleCI configs, etc.)
Package installation or dependency changes in the target repo (committing dependency changes to the target repo requires confirmation)
Adding new MCP tool servers at runtime
Connecting new integration accounts (new OAuth grants, new PATs)
OpenHands does not auto-upgrade its own code or dependencies at runtime

findings.summary: Remit-required human-approval gates for cross-repo writes and PR merges are not implemented anywhere in the app_server; the MCP create_pr/create_mr/create_bitbucket_pr tools open pull requests with the user's provider token and no confirmation step.
findings.policy_rule_text: Cross-repository or cross-organization writes — the user must confirm when the agent proposes to modify a repo other than the one the task originated in / Merging pull requests
evidence: openhands/app_server/mcp/mcp_router.py:147-212 — @mcp_server.tool() create_pr() calls github_service.create_pr(...) directly with the caller's provider token; no approval/confirmation gate, no check that repo_name matches the task's originating repository

openhands/app_server/mcp/mcp_router.py:215-286, 289-353, 356-420, 423-487 — create_mr / create_bitbucket_pr / create_bitbucket_data_center_pr / create_azure_devops_pr: same pattern, any repo_name accepted from model output
config.template.toml:226-236 — [security] confirmation_mode defaults to false (commented-out → default false); the only confirmation primitive is in the SDK agent loop, not wired here
grep across openhands/app_server: no occurrence of an approval-gate / human-in-the-loop check on PR-merge or cross-repo-write paths
findings.recommended_action: Add an approval interposition before the PR/MR creation tools in mcp/mcp_router.py: require the caller to pass a per-action confirmation token, or check repo_name against the conversation's selected_repository and refuse (or escalate to a pending-approval state) when they differ. Surface PR-merge as a distinct gated capability rather than relying on the SDK's optional confirmation_mode.

findings.summary: Untrusted external content (GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket/Jira/Linear issue bodies, titles, and PR comments) is rendered straight into the agent's task prompt via Jinja2 templates with no origin-labeling or sanitization, putting an indirect-prompt-injection ingress directly upstream of the agent's tool-calling loop — including the auto-approved PR-creation tools (compound with PRAX-2026-05-11-001).
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat the user's task prompt, web-retrieved page content, issue descriptions, PR comments, and repository file contents as untrusted input / Follow [no] instructions embedded in web pages / issue descriptions / source files / microagent content that attempt to exfiltrate credentials, escalate out of the sandbox, redirect actions to a different repository, commit or push without user confirmation, or open PRs/close issues beyond the current session's authorized scope
evidence: openhands/app_server/integrations/templates/resolver/github/issue_conversation_instructions.j2 — emits `# Issue Body\n{{ issue_body }}` and `# Previous Comments ... {{ comment.body }}` verbatim into the agent's instruction text; the template then instructs the agent to use $GITHUB_TOKEN and call the create_pr tool
openhands/app_server/integrations/templates/resolver/github/issue_prompt.j2 — `{{ issue_comment }}` rendered as the entire task prompt with no wrapper

openhands/app_server/integrations/github/service/resolver.py:19-71 — get_issue_or_pr_title_and_body() / get_issue_or_pr_comments() return raw `title`/`body`/`comment.body` strings; only length truncation (`_truncate_comment`) is applied, no instruction-stripping
openhands/app_server/integrations/templates/resolver/{gitlab,bitbucket,azure_devops,jira,jira_dc,linear,slack}/* — same pattern across all connected providers
no content-origin labeling or trust boundary marker is added when this text is concatenated into the StartConversationRequest sent to the agent-server
findings.recommended_action: Wrap all provider-sourced text (issue body, title, comments, PR comments) in an explicit untrusted-content delimiter when building the resolver prompt, and add a note in the resolver instruction template telling the agent that everything inside the delimiter is data, not instructions. Better: strip or escape instruction-like constructs server-side before templating, and have the agent-server treat resolver-origin text as a distinct lower-trust context channel.

findings.summary: Remit-required confirmation gates for destructive git operations (force-push, branch delete, history rewrite), CI/workflow file edits, and committing dependency changes have no corresponding control in the app_server — these are left entirely to the SDK agent loop, which this repo neither configures nor enforces.
findings.policy_rule_text: Execute destructive git operations (force-push, branch delete, history rewrite) without user confirmation / CI/CD or workflow file edits (.github/workflows/*, Jenkinsfiles, CircleCI configs, etc.) [require confirmation] / Package installation or dependency changes in the target repo [committing dependency changes requires confirmation]
evidence: openhands/app_server/app_conversation/app_conversation_service_base.py:317-381 — clone_or_init_git_repo() only invokes git clone / checkout (with shlex.quote); there is no path-pattern guard on subsequent agent-driven git operations because those happen inside the SDK agent loop, not here
grep across openhands/app_server: no occurrence of a deny/confirm rule for `push --force`, `branch -D`, `rebase`, `.github/workflows`, Jenkinsfile, or dependency-manifest paths

config.template.toml:223-236 — [security] section exposes only `confirmation_mode` (default false) and `security_analyzer = "llm"` (default), both consumed by the SDK; no per-command/per-path policy file
findings.recommended_action: Either (a) document explicitly that these gates are enforced by openhands-sdk and pin/verify the SDK version that contains them, or (b) add a server-side policy layer (consulted by the agent-server before high-impact git/file actions) that denies force-push / branch-delete / history-rewrite and requires confirmation for edits under .github/workflows/*, CI config files, and dependency manifests. Do not rely on the default-off confirmation_mode.

findings.summary: In OSS/local mode the entire /api/v1/* surface — conversations, sandboxes, secrets, settings, git integrations — is unauthenticated: DefaultUserAuth.get_user_id() returns None and get_dependencies() adds no auth dependency unless the SESSION_API_KEY env var is set; anyone who can reach the port can start sandboxed code-execution conversations with the operator's credentials.
findings.policy_rule_text: Allow one session's state, memory, or credentials to leak into another session (tenant isolation in cloud and enterprise deployments)
evidence: openhands/app_server/utils/dependencies.py:9-32 — get_dependencies() returns [] unless `SESSION_API_KEY` is set in env, or app_mode == SAAS (and even then the SAAS branch only documents X-Access-Token for OpenAPI, not enforce it)
openhands/app_server/user_auth/default_user_auth.py:32-42 — get_user_id() / get_user_email() / get_access_token() all return None ('does not support multi tenancy')

openhands/app_server/middleware.py:34-53 — LocalhostCORSMiddleware.is_allowed_origin(): when no permitted_cors_origins are configured it returns True for ANY origin (logs a warning) — CORS protection effectively disabled by default
openhands/app_server/app.py:81-86 — only LocalhostCORSMiddleware, CacheControlMiddleware, and a 10-req/s in-memory rate limiter are attached; no auth middleware in the OSS path
findings.recommended_action: Ship a non-empty default for SESSION_API_KEY (or auto-generate one to ~/.openhands on first run and require it), bind the dev server to 127.0.0.1 rather than 0.0.0.0 by default, and change LocalhostCORSMiddleware to allow only localhost origins when none are configured instead of allow-any. Document loudly that exposing the port without SESSION_API_KEY grants unauthenticated code execution.

findings.summary: The Docker sandbox — the agent's declared isolation boundary — is started with weak hardening (no read-only rootfs, no cap_drop, no seccomp/no-new-privileges, optional host-network mode and /dev/kvm passthrough), and docker-compose mounts /var/run/docker.sock into the app container, so a container escape or a compromised app process reaches the host Docker daemon.
findings.policy_rule_text: Execute all agent-generated code inside the sandboxed runtime — never on the host directly / Execute [no] agent-generated code on the host OS outside the sandboxed runtime
evidence: openhands/app_server/sandbox/docker_sandbox_service.py:461-487 — docker_client.containers.run(...) sets only `init=True`; no `read_only`, `cap_drop`, `security_opt`, `pids_limit`, `mem_limit`, or user-namespace remapping; `network_mode='host'` when AGENT_SERVER_USE_HOST_NETWORK is set; `devices=['/dev/kvm:/dev/kvm:rwm']` when SANDBOX_KVM_ENABLED

docker-compose.yml:18-21 — `volumes: - /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock` and `- ~/.openhands:/.openhands` and `- ${WORKSPACE_BASE:-$PWD/workspace}:/opt/workspace_base` mounted into the openhands app container

openhands/app_server/sandbox/process_sandbox_service.py:109-158 — the 'process' / 'local' runtime spawns the agent-server as a plain subprocess.Popen on the host with no isolation at all (RUNTIME=local|process); not the default, but a supported config
config.template.toml:341-343 — [kubernetes] privileged = false is the only hardening toggle exposed, and it's for docker-in-docker
findings.recommended_action: Harden the Docker sandbox launch in DockerSandboxService.start_sandbox: add cap_drop=['ALL'] with a minimal add-back, security_opt=['no-new-privileges'], a pids_limit and mem_limit, and read_only=True with explicit tmpfs/workspace mounts. Default AGENT_SERVER_USE_HOST_NETWORK off and warn hard when enabled. Avoid mounting docker.sock into the app container in the published compose file (use a rootless or socket-proxy pattern), and treat the local/process runtime as explicitly unsafe.

findings.summary: The remit's input-validation-and-output-filtering boundary ('validate tool-argument shapes, clamp numeric parameters, reject shell commands that reach outside the sandbox') is not present in this repo — tool-argument validation and exec gating live entirely in the SDK agent loop, and the MCP tools defined here accept raw model-supplied repo names and branch names with only Pydantic type coercion.
findings.policy_rule_text: Apply input validation and output filtering at the boundary where LLM output drives tool calls — specifically, validate tool-argument shapes, clamp numeric parameters, and reject shell commands that reach outside the sandbox
evidence: openhands/app_server/mcp/mcp_router.py:147-487 — the create_pr / create_mr / create_bitbucket_pr tools take `repo_name`, `source_branch`, `target_branch`, `title`, `body`, `labels` as plain Annotated[str] from model output; no allow-list, no canonicalization of repo_name, branch names not run through git check-ref-format here

openhands/app_server/utils/git.py:9-32 — ensure_valid_git_branch_name() exists and uses `git check-ref-format`, but it is only called in clone_or_init_git_repo (app_conversation_service_base.py:369), not in the MCP PR-creation path

no shell-command interposition exists in the app_server; the 'reject shell commands that reach outside the sandbox' rule depends on the openhands-sdk / openhands-agent-server packages, which are out of scope here
findings.recommended_action: Validate repo_name against the conversation's known repositories (or a provider-specific format regex) before calling the integration service in mcp/mcp_router.py, and call ensure_valid_git_branch_name() on source_branch/target_branch there too. Confirm and document where the exec-argument and path-traversal validation lives in the SDK, and pin the SDK version that contains it.

findings.summary: Host-filesystem paths are mounted into the agent's reach: docker-compose binds the host CWD/workspace and ~/.openhands (which holds secrets.json, .keys, .jwt_secret) into containers, and the SANDBOX_VOLUMES / --mount-cwd path lets arbitrary host directories be mounted into the sandbox with no allow-list.
findings.policy_rule_text: Read, write, or reference [no] files on the host filesystem outside the sandbox workspace
evidence: docker-compose.yml:18-21 — `- ~/.openhands:/.openhands` and `- ${WORKSPACE_BASE:-$PWD/workspace}:/opt/workspace_base` mounted into the app container; ~/.openhands holds secrets.json, .keys, .jwt_secret (see openhands/app_server/secrets/file_secrets_store.py:15 and utils/encryption_key.py:53-63)

openhands/app_server/config.py:358-385 — SANDBOX_VOLUMES env var is split on ',' and each `host_path:container_path[:mode]` is turned into a VolumeMount and passed to DockerSandboxService with no validation that host_path is inside an approved root

openhands/app_server/sandbox/docker_sandbox_service.py:438-445 — `volumes = {mount.host_path: {'bind': mount.container_path, 'mode': mount.mode} for mount in self.mounts}` — mounts are applied verbatim
findings.recommended_action: Restrict SANDBOX_VOLUMES/--mount-cwd to an operator-configured allow-list of host roots and reject paths that resolve outside it (and reject the persistence dir). Do not mount ~/.openhands into the sandbox container; if the app container needs it, mount a narrowed subdirectory. Document that secrets/keys are file-backed under the persistence dir and gate filesystem access to that dir.

findings.summary: Repo-supplied configuration files — .openhands/setup.sh, .openhands/pre-commit.sh, .openhands/hooks.json, and org-level .openhands skill repos — are executed/loaded at conversation start with no review, so a connected (or attacker-influenced) repository can run arbitrary commands inside the sandbox and inject content into the agent's context the moment a task is started against it.
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat the user's task prompt, web-retrieved page content, issue descriptions, PR comments, and repository file contents as untrusted input
evidence: openhands/app_server/app_conversation/app_conversation_service_base.py:383-400 — maybe_run_setup_script(): `chmod +x {setup_script} && source {setup_script}` where setup_script = `{project_dir}/.openhands/setup.sh` (the cloned repo's file), 600s timeout, no review
openhands/app_server/app_conversation/app_conversation_service_base.py:402-457 — maybe_setup_git_hooks(): installs the repo's `.openhands/pre-commit.sh` as `.git/hooks/pre-commit`

openhands/app_server/app_conversation/hook_loader.py:19-101 — loads `.openhands/hooks.json` from the repo and turns it into a HookConfig; HookDefinitionResponse.type can be 'command' (app_conversation_models.py:282-296)
openhands/app_server/app_conversation/skill_loader.py:127-241 — org-level skills are pulled from `{org}/.openhands` / `{org}/openhands-config` repos and merged into the agent's context
findings.recommended_action: Treat .openhands/setup.sh, .openhands/pre-commit.sh, and .openhands/hooks.json as untrusted: gate their execution behind explicit operator opt-in per repository (or a first-run confirmation), and surface a diff of what will run. Constrain org-skill loading to repos the operator has explicitly allow-listed. These run inside the sandbox so the blast radius is bounded, but the implicit trust granted to repo contents contradicts the remit's 'repository file contents as untrusted input' rule.

findings.summary: Provider tokens and the master encryption key are persisted as plaintext-readable files under the persistence dir (secrets.json, .keys, .jwt_secret) rather than in an OS keychain or vault; the remit's 'do not cache long-lived credentials in LLM-reachable memory' is met for the agent path (LookupSecret references) but the at-rest storage is file-based.
findings.policy_rule_text: Verify OAuth tokens, integration PATs, and session tokens on every request to integration APIs — do not cache long-lived credentials in LLM-reachable memory
evidence: openhands/app_server/secrets/file_secrets_store.py:13-34 — FileSecretsStore writes `secrets.json` via file_store.write with `model_dump_json(context={'expose_secrets': True})` — provider tokens and custom secrets in cleartext JSON

openhands/app_server/utils/encryption_key.py:50-86 — master encryption keys persisted to `{persistence_dir}/.keys` as JSON with `context={'expose_secrets': True}`, and a legacy `.jwt_secret` plaintext file is read if present

Positive offset: openhands/app_server/app_conversation/live_status_app_conversation_service.py:939-961 — agent receives a LookupSecret (time-limited JWS token, fetched on demand via /api/v1/webhooks/secrets) rather than the literal token when web_url is configured
findings.recommended_action: Where an OS keychain is available (macOS Keychain, libsecret), store provider tokens and the master key there instead of .keys / secrets.json; otherwise ensure the persistence dir is created mode 0700 and document the file-based storage as a known limitation. Verify file permissions on .keys, .jwt_secret, and secrets.json at startup.

findings.summary: Per-session caps are partially implemented at the orchestration layer (max_concurrent_conversations=3, max_num_sandboxes=5, conversation_max_age_seconds, 10 req/s rate limiter) and max_budget_per_task defaults to 0.0 = unlimited; the remit's wall-clock and step-count caps with clean halt depend on max_iterations/budget being enforced inside the SDK agent loop, not here.
findings.policy_rule_text: Enforce per-session wall-clock and step-count caps; halt the agent cleanly when exceeded
evidence: config.template.toml:53-56 — `#max_budget_per_task = 0.0` (0.0 means no limit), `#max_iterations = 500` — both commented out; the budget default is unlimited
config.template.toml:91-95 — `#max_concurrent_conversations = 3`, `#conversation_max_age_seconds = 864000` (10 days)
openhands/app_server/sandbox/docker_sandbox_service.py:578-581 — max_num_sandboxes default 5
openhands/app_server/app.py:83-86 — InMemoryRateLimiter(requests=10, seconds=1) on all routes
openhands/app_server/middleware.py:75-110 — the rate limiter is purely in-memory and per-client-IP; no durable budget/cost ceiling is enforced in this repo
findings.recommended_action: Set a non-zero default for max_budget_per_task (denial-of-wallet protection), and document/verify that max_iterations and any wall-clock cap are enforced by the agent-server with a clean halt. Consider a per-conversation server-side wall-clock timeout in the app_server itself as defence in depth.

findings.summary: The 'never commit secrets to any git branch' rule is backed only by the installed pre-commit.sh hook (which a repo's own hook or a --no-verify can bypass) and downstream redaction; there is no server-side scan of the agent's diffs/commits for secrets before push.
findings.policy_rule_text: Commit [no] secrets, credentials, tokens, or `.env` contents to any git branch
evidence: openhands/app_server/app_conversation/git/pre-commit.sh — the only secret-prevention mechanism on the commit path; openhands/app_server/app_conversation/app_conversation_service_base.py:443-457 installs it as `.git/hooks/pre-commit`, but git commit --no-verify bypasses pre-commit hooks and the agent has shell access inside the sandbox

no server-side commit/diff secret scanner is invoked before the agent pushes; the only redaction (utils/logger.py SensitiveDataFilter, sdk.utils.redact) protects logs and error messages, not committed content
findings.recommended_action: Add a server-side secret scan of the agent's staged changes (or of the push payload) using a denylist of token patterns and the user's known secret names, and refuse the push when a match is found — independent of the in-sandbox pre-commit hook. At minimum, detect --no-verify usage by the agent and flag it.

findings.summary: Cross-session isolation relies on Python module-global in-memory stores in the sandbox services (a process-wide _processes dict, mutable singletons via the injector) and on every SQL query remembering to filter by user_id; a missed filter or a shared process is a tenant-isolation gap, and the OSS DefaultUserAuth has no user_id at all so isolation collapses to per-process.
findings.policy_rule_text: Allow [no] one session's state, memory, or credentials to leak into another session (tenant isolation in cloud and enterprise deployments)
evidence: openhands/app_server/sandbox/process_sandbox_service.py:62-63 — `_processes: dict[str, ProcessInfo] = {}` is a module-global shared across all requests/users in the process; get_sandbox_by_session_api_key iterates it

openhands/app_server/app_conversation/sql_app_conversation_start_task_service.py:99-101, 167-169, 199-201, 224-226 — user scoping is `if self.user_id: query = query.where(... created_by_user_id == self.user_id)`; when user_id is None (OSS) the filter is silently skipped

openhands/app_server/sandbox/session_auth.py:103-139 — validate_session_key_ownership() does enforce sandbox-owner == caller, which is a real positive; but it's only invoked on the endpoints that opt in
findings.recommended_action: Make user-scoping non-optional: have the SQL services require a non-null tenant key in multi-tenant deployments and fail closed when it's missing. Replace the module-global _processes store with a per-user keyed structure or move it behind the same admin-context boundary as the Docker lookup. Audit every app_server query for an unconditional created_by_user_id filter.

findings.summary: Dependency hygiene is mixed: uv.lock and poetry.lock provide full hashed lockfiles and the OpenHands-owned packages are exactly pinned, but the pyproject.toml manifest uses >= floors for most deps and leaves several wholly unconstrained (anthropic[vertex], boto3, docker, fastapi, numpy, uvicorn, termcolor, toml), and MCP stdio_servers config installs servers via npx/uvx with no provenance.
findings.policy_rule_text: OpenHands does not auto-upgrade its own code or dependencies at runtime
evidence: pyproject.toml dependencies[] — `anthropic[vertex]`, `boto3`, `docker`, `fastapi`, `numpy`, `uvicorn`, `termcolor`, `toml`, `pexpect`, `psutil` have no version constraint; most others are `>=` floors only; OpenHands packages pinned exactly (`openhands-sdk==1.21.1`, `openhands-agent-server==1.21.1`, `openhands-tools==1.21.1`, `openhands-aci==0.3.3`)
uv.lock and poetry.lock present at repo root (full lockfiles with hashes) — partially mitigates the loose manifest

config.template.toml:371-383 — `[mcp] stdio_servers` examples use `npx @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem` and `uvx mcp-server-fetch` with no integrity pin; docker-compose.yml:11 pins `AGENT_SERVER_IMAGE_TAG=1.19.1-python`
no SBOM / ML-BOM artifact and no documented dependency-review process in the staged workspace
findings.recommended_action: Tighten pyproject.toml to upper-bounded ranges (or exact pins) for the unconstrained deps, keep uv.lock/poetry.lock as the source of truth in CI, and publish an SBOM. For MCP stdio_servers, recommend pinned package versions and document a provenance-check step.

findings.summary: No adversarial-testing or red-team evidence is present in the staged workspace — no injection-test fixtures, no red-team report, no postmortem showing a found issue drove an architectural change — so the agent's resistance to prompt injection via the resolver/web/repo ingress points is unverified at this layer.
findings.policy_rule_text: Treat the user's task prompt, web-retrieved page content, issue descriptions, PR comments, and repository file contents as untrusted input
evidence: Staged workspace contains the openhands/ Python package + config.template.toml + docker-compose.yml only; no `tests/`, no red-team artifacts, no `*adversarial*`, `*injection*`, `*redteam*` files in scope

Security-awareness signals exist (openhands/app_server/constants.py BLOCKED_SECRET_NAMES rationale comments, 'Security Note:' docstrings in sandbox/session_auth.py, redaction utils), but none of these is evidence of adversarial testing changing the design
OpenHands is a public, widely-used coding agent and a frequent security-research target; absence of red-team evidence in-scope is a gap, not proof none exists
findings.recommended_action: If adversarial testing exists (it likely does in the broader repo / SDK), surface it: keep injection-resistance test fixtures in this repo's tests/ exercising the resolver prompt path, the web-fetch path, and repo-file ingestion, and link postmortems where findings changed the architecture. If it does not exist, stand up an indirect-injection red-team exercise against the resolver and browser ingress points before the next release.

findings.summary: DEBUG_LLM mode streams full prompts/responses (and litellm verbose output) to logs; it is guarded by an interactive 'type y to confirm' prompt and a warning, but that gate is bypassable in non-interactive deployments (input() on a closed stdin) and the LlmFileHandler writes prompt/response files when LOG_TO_FILE is on.
findings.policy_rule_text: Leak [no] API keys or session tokens into logs, error messages, or LLM context
evidence: openhands/app_server/utils/logger.py:43-58 — DEBUG_LLM triggers `litellm.set_verbose = True` after an `input('Type y to confirm...')` prompt; in a non-TTY/non-interactive context input() raises EOFError or reads empty, so behaviour depends on how that exception is handled

openhands/app_server/utils/logger.py:457-525 — LlmFileHandler writes `prompt_NNN.log` / `response_NNN.log` under LOG_DIR/llm/ when LOG_TO_FILE; not redacted by SensitiveDataFilter (separate logger, llm_formatter)
Positive offset: the main openhands logger has SensitiveDataFilter on every handler (logger.py:401-420) and LiteLLM loggers are disabled by default (logger.py:436-438)
findings.recommended_action: Make DEBUG_LLM impossible to enable without an explicit, non-interactive opt-in flag (env var that must equal a specific token), and run the llm_prompt_logger/llm_response_logger output through the same redaction filter as the main logger.

positives.title: Running-state-gated session-key authentication for sandbox endpoints
positives.description: validate_session_key() rejects session API keys for any sandbox not in RUNNING state and (in SAAS mode) requires an owning user; validate_session_key_ownership() additionally checks sandbox-owner == caller, limiting damage from leaked keys.

positives.title: Secret-name validation wired into the API-secrets path
positives.description: validate_secret_name() / validate_secrets_dict() block reserved env-var names (webhook/CORS/LLM-control variables) and enforce count/length limits; they are actually called from the conversation-start path, not just declared.

positives.title: Time-limited LookupSecret token references instead of static credentials in the agent
positives.description: When a web URL is configured, the agent receives a LookupSecret pointing at /api/v1/webhooks/secrets with a short-lived JWS access token rather than the literal provider token, matching the remit's 'do not cache long-lived credentials in LLM-reachable memory'.

positives.title: Pervasive log-side secret redaction
positives.description: SensitiveDataFilter (env-var-derived values + a curated pattern list) plus the SDK redact utils (redact_api_key_literals, redact_text_secrets, redact_url_params) are attached to every logger handler; LiteLLM loggers are disabled by default; error details surfaced to users are redacted (task.detail = redact_text_secrets(redact_api_key_literals(...))).

positives.title: Durable, structured event store for conversation actions
positives.description: EventService persists every conversation Event (filesystem / S3 / GCS backends), the webhook callback chain records conversation lifecycle and stats, and structured JSON logging (LOG_JSON via python-json-logger) is available — enough to reconstruct what a session did.

positives.title: Shell-injection-safe git invocation and branch-name validation
positives.description: clone_or_init_git_repo() quotes the remote URL, directory name, and branch with shlex.quote, and ensure_valid_git_branch_name() validates branch names via `git check-ref-format` before using them in a checkout command.

positives.title: Pinned OpenHands-owned packages and agent-server image, plus full lockfiles
positives.description: openhands-sdk / openhands-agent-server / openhands-tools / openhands-aci are exactly version-pinned in pyproject.toml, the agent-server sandbox image tag is pinned in docker-compose, and uv.lock + poetry.lock provide hashed lockfiles for the full dependency tree.

positives.title: Explicit deprecation discipline for the legacy V0 surface
positives.description: The legacy openhands/server/* modules and user_auth/default_user_auth.py carry clear 'Legacy-V0 / deprecated, scheduled for removal' banners pointing to the V1 app_server and SDK, reducing the chance of accidental use of the old web server.

log_files.no_logs_note: The staged workspace is source code, not a running deployment, so no runtime log files are present; OpenHands does log at runtime (TimedRotatingFileHandler 'openhands.log' under <repo>/logs when LOG_TO_FILE, plus a persistent event store under the persistence dir), so Monitor Continuously is scored on the logging code rather than captured artifacts — see PRAX-2026-05-11-015 for the DEBUG_LLM caveat.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. OpenHands has a coherent, security-conscious orchestration layer with real controls — running-state-gated session-key auth, wired secret-name validation, time-limited credential references handed to the agent, a durable structured event store, and thorough log-side redaction — but the most consequential promises in its remit are unenforced at the layer Praxa can see: the human-approval gates (cross-repo writes, PR merges, force-push/history-rewrite, CI-file edits, dependency commits) have no interposition and the PR-creation MCP tools fire with no confirmation, while untrusted issue/PR/web/repo content flows straight into the agent's task prompt. The agentic core that would back input-validation, exec-gating, and step caps lives in the pinned openhands-sdk package outside this repo, so those rules are 'partial / enforcement-not-possible-here' rather than verified. Combined with no in-scope adversarial-testing evidence and an OSS mode that ships with no API authentication, the framework-wide picture is a maturing system whose strongest area (Monitor / Supply Chain at Established) is offset by an Implement-Zero-Trust surface that is Partial at best.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Single declared mission with a small set of agent kinds ('default', 'plan') and config flags that gate the tool set (enable_browsing/enable_cmd/enable_jupyter/enable_editor, disabled_microagents/disabled_skills); the inherent breadth of 'autonomous software engineering' (arbitrary code execution is the point) is the accepted gap, and per-session budget/iteration caps are partial (max_budget_per_task default 0.0 = unlimited) — see PRAX-2026-05-11-010.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Untrusted external content — issue bodies, titles, PR comments, web pages, repo files, repo-supplied setup.sh/hooks.json/org skills — enters the agent's task prompt and execution path via Jinja2 templates and conversation-start scripts with no origin-labeling or sanitization in this repo (PRAX-2026-05-11-002, -008); the templates do separate 'issue body' sections, showing awareness, but there is no trust boundary enforced at this layer.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real controls exist and are wired (session-key auth gated on RUNNING sandboxes, validate_secret_name/validate_secrets_dict, LookupSecret token references, shlex.quote on git args, per-user/per-sandbox concurrency caps, a 10 req/s rate limiter), but the remit's human-approval gates are unimplemented and the create_pr/create_mr MCP tools open PRs with no confirmation (PRAX-2026-05-11-001, -003); OSS mode has no API auth and allow-any CORS by default (PRAX-2026-05-11-004); tool-argument/exec validation lives in the out-of-repo SDK (PRAX-2026-05-11-006); Docker sandbox hardening is thin and docker.sock is mounted into the app container (PRAX-2026-05-11-005).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: uv.lock + poetry.lock provide full hashed lockfiles, the OpenHands-owned packages and the agent-server sandbox image are exactly pinned, and there is a deprecation discipline for legacy modules; but the pyproject.toml manifest uses >= floors and leaves several deps wholly unconstrained, MCP stdio_servers install via npx/uvx with no provenance pin, and there is no SBOM or documented dependency-review process in scope (PRAX-2026-05-11-013), and secrets/keys are stored as files rather than in a vault (PRAX-2026-05-11-009).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing artifacts, injection-test fixtures, red-team reports, or postmortems-driving-design-change are present in the staged workspace (tests/ was out of scope); the codebase shows security awareness (BLOCKED_SECRET_NAMES rationale, 'Security Note:' docstrings, redaction utils) but that is not evidence of an adversarial feedback loop — PRAX-2026-05-11-014.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Structured logging with pervasive secret-redaction filters on every handler, optional JSON log output, a TimedRotatingFileHandler, a durable conversation event store (filesystem/S3/GCS) recording action-level events, and a webhook callback chain recording conversation lifecycle/stats/terminal-state analytics (consent-gated); gaps are no alerting on high-impact actions, no anomaly detection, and the DEBUG_LLM prompt-dump path whose interactive gate is bypassable in non-interactive deployments (PRAX-2026-05-11-015).
